text: The surgeon adjusts the dissection plane to remain in the preperitoneal space during a RTAP left indirect hernia repair. The flap is extended laterally, and the surgeon corrects the plane to avoid entering the pre-transverse salus or retro-rectus space, ensuring proper positioning above the arcuate line.

Below the arcuate line, the surgeon performs medial dissection, exposing the bone while avoiding creating holes. The peritoneum appears thin, requiring careful handling to prevent tearing. The surgeon sweeps the cord structures off the indirect hernia sac, which is visible and not large, intending to reduce the entire sac rather than amputate it. The tissue planes are clearly visible, and the edge of the sac is identified.

The surgeon prioritizes the cord structures, creating space laterally and medially for mesh placement. A 12x16 ProGrip mesh is prepared to be placed in the flap, while the assistant switches the scissors for a needle driver.
The surgeon ensures the mesh is positioned flat without any bowing, folding, or clamshelling, before proceeding to close the surgical flap.
